user_priorities: Algorithm correctness — the mastery algorithm must be deterministic and bug-free; a broken transition could sabotage exam preparation
Atomic build tasks — every task must be completable by a less-capable model without ambiguity (CON-006)
Zero ongoing cost — permanent deployment on free tier only (CON-002)
Offline first — full app functionality after first load, no network required (FR-020, NFR-009)
Zero manual infrastructure setup — all provisioning by Claude Code (CON-003)
Fast initial load — <2s to first question on first visit (NFR-001)
hardest_constraints: React + Vite is locked — no alternative framework evaluation possible (CON-001)
Every build task must be atomic enough for a less-capable model (CON-006)
Zero paid infrastructure — Upstash free tier + Vercel Hobby only (CON-002)
frontend-design skill runs before any component code (CON-007)
critical_success_factors: 5-state algorithm transitions are deterministic and match the SSOT exactly
App opens to a quiz question with zero interactions after first load (SC-001)
Full offline functionality after one online visit (SC-002)
PWA installable on iOS Safari and Android Chrome (SC-006)

pros.relevance: Smaller bundle, fewer upgrade surface areas, no library-specific patterns to learn
pros.user_alignment: You care about zero ongoing cost and simplicity — fewer deps means fewer maintenance liabilities

pros.point: useReducer maps naturally to the dispatch-action pattern of a state machine
pros.relevance: The 5-state mastery machine has well-defined actions (ANSWER_CORRECT, ANSWER_WRONG, FAST_TAP_DETECTED) — useReducer handles these cleanly
pros.user_alignment: The algorithm's transition logic fits the reducer pattern philosophically — each action case maps to a state transition rule

pros.point: vite-plugin-pwa handles iOS Safari and Android PWA edge cases automatically
pros.relevance: You need offline to work on iOS Safari (your primary mobile platform) — vite-plugin-pwa is battle-tested for this
pros.user_alignment: You mentioned iOS Safari as primary target in the SSOT — vite-plugin-pwa's iOS manifest and SW handling is correct out of the box

pros.point: Pure React primitives — fewer concepts for a less-capable model to understand
pros.relevance: Context + useReducer are React core — no library-specific API to explain in atomic task specs
pros.user_alignment: CON-006 requires atomic tasks completable without ambiguity — fewer APIs means fewer things to explain

cons.point: No TypeScript — algorithm bugs surface at runtime, not compile time
cons.relevance: The 5-state machine tracks streak counts, reversal arrays, session ID sets, and mastery timestamps per question — these have complex interdependencies that TypeScript catches
cons.mitigation: Comprehensive unit tests for every state transition can partially compensate, but test coverage must be exhaustive for 5×N state paths
cons.user_consideration: You're building a study tool for a medical certification — a bug in the mastery algorithm that promotes a question incorrectly directly impacts exam readiness. TypeScript is cheaper insurance than debugging at runtime.

cons.point: Context re-renders all consumers on state change — potential performance issue with 131-question state tree
cons.relevance: Every answer submission updates the global store, which may trigger re-renders in tab nav, progress bar, and analytics components simultaneously
cons.mitigation: React.memo and useMemo on expensive components; split Context into 'question state' and 'UI state' contexts
cons.user_consideration: You require <300ms tab switch (NFR-003) — this is achievable with Context but requires careful memoization that adds task complexity

cons.point: Custom localStorage serialization code is required — adds ~150-200 lines that need their own atomic tasks
cons.relevance: Persist middleware from Zustand eliminates this entirely; Context/useReducer requires writing, testing, and migrating this manually
cons.mitigation: Write a reusable storage adapter module early and test it thoroughly
cons.user_consideration: CON-006 (atomic build tasks) means this storage adapter needs its own precisely-defined tasks — it's solvable but adds scope

cons.point: No devtools for state inspection — algorithm debugging requires console.log or browser DevTools manually
cons.relevance: During development of the mastery algorithm, inspecting the question state tree requires manual tooling
cons.mitigation: React DevTools shows Context state; acceptable for development

analyses.fit_summary: Approach A is a workable, dependency-light choice that uses only React core primitives. The primary concern is algorithm safety — without TypeScript, the 5-state mastery machine's complex state transitions rely entirely on runtime testing rather than compile-time verification. The custom localStorage code also adds implementation surface. For a study tool where algorithm correctness directly impacts certification readiness, this is a meaningful risk.
analyses.best_for: Projects where minimizing dependencies is the top priority and algorithm complexity is low
analyses.worst_for: Projects with complex state machines where compile-time correctness is important

pros.point: TypeScript discriminated unions enforce exhaustive handling of all 5 algorithm states at compile time
pros.relevance: type QuestionState = 'new' | 'active' | 'near_mastery' | 'mastered' | 'maintenance' — every switch statement on this type gets a TypeScript error if any case is unhandled
pros.user_alignment: You said 'algorithm correctness is user responsibility' for content, but the algorithm itself must be correct. TypeScript is the first safety net — free to apply, eliminates an entire class of bugs.

pros.point: Zustand persist middleware eliminates all custom localStorage serialization code
pros.relevance: Instead of writing a storage adapter, migrate functions, and serialization tests, you configure: persist(store, { name: 'fap_store' }) — the library handles the rest
pros.user_alignment: CON-006 requires atomic tasks — fewer custom utility modules means fewer atomic tasks and less implementation surface for a less-capable model

pros.point: Selector-based subscriptions prevent unnecessary re-renders without manual memoization
pros.relevance: useStore(s => s.questions[id].state) only re-renders when that specific question's state changes — NFR-003 (<300ms tab switch) is easier to achieve
pros.user_alignment: NFR-003 requires fast tab switching — Zustand's granular subscriptions deliver this without the memoization boilerplate Context requires

pros.point: TypeScript interfaces make atomic task specifications unambiguous for a less-capable model
pros.relevance: A task that specifies 'add field sessionIds: string[] to QuestionRecord interface in src/types/algorithm.ts' is zero-ambiguity. A JS task saying 'add an array of session IDs to each question object' leaves structure undefined.
pros.user_alignment: CON-006 is your hardest execution constraint — TypeScript is the best tool for making tasks atomic and unambiguous

pros.point: vite-plugin-pwa generates correct iOS-specific manifest fields and SW update lifecycle automatically
pros.relevance: iOS Safari requires specific meta tags, display modes, and SW registration timing — vite-plugin-pwa handles all of these correctly out of the box
pros.user_alignment: SC-006 requires PWA installable on iOS Safari — this is the highest-risk PWA requirement. vite-plugin-pwa is the proven solution.

pros.point: Zustand schema migration via version field is well-documented for localStorage persistence
pros.relevance: If you need to add a field to QuestionRecord post-deployment, Zustand's migrate option handles this cleanly
pros.user_alignment: The app is intended to be permanent — schema evolution over time is a realistic concern

cons.point: TypeScript configuration (tsconfig.json) must be set up correctly before first build task
cons.relevance: Incorrect strictness settings can make TypeScript less useful or generate spurious errors; this must be resolved in the architecture phase
cons.mitigation: Use Vite's official TypeScript + React template as the tsconfig baseline — it's well-tested and widely used
cons.user_consideration: This is a one-time setup cost in the architecture phase, not an ongoing burden

cons.point: Zustand persist requires a migration function if localStorage schema changes post-deployment
cons.relevance: Adding a new field to the persisted store requires a version bump and migration in zustand's persist config
cons.mitigation: Document the migration pattern in the architecture phase so it's a known atomic task pattern, not a surprise
cons.user_consideration: You'll likely iterate on the question state schema during development — establish the migration pattern early

cons.mitigation: Bundle will be cached by service worker on install — first load impact is minimal, repeat loads are zero
cons.user_consideration: NFR-001 requires <2s first load — 3.5KB additional gzipped payload is negligible on any mobile connection

analyses.fit_summary: Approach B is the strongest fit for this project. TypeScript directly serves the algorithm correctness requirement and the atomic build task constraint simultaneously. Zustand persist eliminates the most error-prone custom code (localStorage serialization). vite-plugin-pwa handles the iOS Safari PWA requirements that represent the highest platform risk. Every con is minor and manageable. This approach produces the most unambiguous atomic task specifications while minimizing custom utility code.
analyses.best_for: Projects with complex business logic, strict atomic task requirements, and mobile PWA as a primary target
analyses.worst_for: Projects where zero additional dependencies is a hard constraint

pros.point: Full control over cache strategy and cache version naming
pros.relevance: You can define exactly which assets are cached and how cache invalidation works
pros.user_alignment: vite-plugin-pwa exposes the same cache configuration — this benefit is available in Approach B without the added complexity

pros.relevance: One fewer Vite plugin in the build chain
pros.user_alignment: This dependency savings is not meaningful — vite-plugin-pwa is stable, widely used, and maintained by the Vite ecosystem team

pros.point: TypeScript and Zustand advantages from Approach B still apply
pros.relevance: Algorithm correctness and atomic task precision are inherited from the same language and state management choices
pros.user_alignment: Same benefits as Approach B for the algorithm and state management concerns

cons.point: Custom service worker must enumerate Vite's hashed output filenames to cache — requires a post-build script
cons.relevance: Vite outputs files like main.a1b2c3d4.js — the service worker must cache these exact names. Without vite-plugin-pwa, a custom script must read the Vite manifest and inject filenames into the SW
cons.mitigation: Write a build script (vite.config plugin or Node.js post-build) that reads vite-manifest.json and writes the asset list to the SW file
cons.user_consideration: This post-build script is itself a non-trivial atomic task — CON-006 requires it to be precisely specified, and it must be re-tested after every Vite version upgrade

cons.point: iOS Safari service worker edge cases must be solved manually
cons.relevance: iOS Safari has specific behaviors: SW update detection, navigation fallback for SPA routing, install prompt absence, cache scope restrictions. Workbox (used by vite-plugin-pwa) handles all of these — a custom SW must solve each independently
cons.mitigation: Study web.dev iOS SW documentation thoroughly; test on physical iOS device at every step
cons.user_consideration: SC-006 requires iOS Safari installable PWA — this is your highest-risk requirement. A custom SW significantly increases the probability of hitting an iOS Safari bug. This could block the entire deployment.

cons.point: Service worker update lifecycle bugs silently serve stale content
cons.relevance: If the SW doesn't correctly call skipWaiting() and clientsClaim(), users see old app versions after deployment
cons.mitigation: Implement update lifecycle correctly on first try; test by deploying two versions and verifying update
cons.user_consideration: You mentioned permanent replacement — SW update bugs could mean the deployed version gets stuck. vite-plugin-pwa handles this correctly by default.

cons.point: Higher implementation complexity with no offsetting benefit over Approach B
cons.relevance: Approach B provides all the same algorithm and state management benefits with a proven PWA solution. Approach C adds PWA risk for no gain.
cons.mitigation: There is no mitigation — use Approach B instead
cons.user_consideration: The vision anchor 'atomic build tasks' and 'permanent replacement' both argue against Approach C. The custom SW is the opposite of atomic — it has environment dependencies, platform quirks, and maintenance burden.

analyses.fit_summary: Approach C inherits Approach B's language and state management advantages but introduces significant unnecessary risk in the service worker layer. The custom SW adds a non-trivial build artifact (post-build manifest script), requires manual handling of iOS Safari's documented gotchas, and offers no benefit over vite-plugin-pwa for this project's requirements. The vision anchor 'atomic build tasks' is directly undermined by the custom SW's complexity and platform dependencies. Approach B is strictly superior.
analyses.best_for: Projects with highly specific caching requirements that cannot be expressed in vite-plugin-pwa configuration
analyses.worst_for: Projects targeting iOS Safari as a primary platform, or any project where development speed and reliability matter more than total control

comparative_insights.recommendation_rationale: Approach B (TypeScript + Zustand + vite-plugin-pwa) wins on every dimension that matters for this project. TypeScript addresses the highest-stakes risk (algorithm correctness). Zustand persist eliminates the most error-prone custom code (localStorage serialization). vite-plugin-pwa is the proven solution for the highest-risk platform requirement (iOS Safari installable PWA). Approach A is viable but introduces algorithm correctness risk that is unacceptable for a medical certification study tool. Approach C adds complexity and iOS Safari risk with no offsetting benefit.
